readable energy units string formatting for plot_interactive & plot_grid","user":{"login":"facero","id":8652898,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2NTI4OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8652898?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero","html_url":"https://github.com/facero","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"**Description** When using data on a large energy range or for the example the Crab dataset, the energy label are not the most readable (e.g. 2.56e8 keV instead of 256 GeV). This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? readable energy units string formatting for plot_interactive & plot_grid","user":{"login":"facero","id":8652898,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2NTI4OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8652898?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero","html_url":"https://github.com/facero","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"**Description** When using data on a large energy range or for the example the Crab dataset, the energy label are not the most readable (e.g. 2.56e8 keV instead of 256 GeV). This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? When using data on a large energy range or for the example the Crab dataset, the energy label are not the most readable (e.g. 2.56e8 keV instead of 256 GeV). This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? When using data on a large energy range or for the example the Crab dataset, the energy label are not the most readable (e.g. 2.56e8 keV instead of 256 GeV). This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. When using data on a large energy range or for the example the Crab dataset, the energy label are not the most readable (e.g. 2.56e8 keV instead of 256 GeV). This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ? {"action":"opened","number":3730,"pull_request":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/pulls/3730","id":831298037,"node_id":"PR_kwDOAJm2KM4xjJn1","html_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/pull/3730","diff_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/pull/3730.diff","patch_url":"https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy/pull/3730.patch","issue_url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/3730","number":3730,"state":"open","locked":false,"title":"Mamba doc update","user":{"login":"facero","id":8652898,"node_id":"MDQ6VXNlcjg2NTI4OTg=","avatar_url":"https://avatars.githubusercontent.com/u/8652898?v=4","gravatar_id":"","url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero","html_url":"https://github.com/facero","followers_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/followers","following_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/following{/other_user}","gists_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/gists{/gist_id}","starred_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/starred{/owner}{/repo}","subscriptions_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/subscriptions","organizations_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/orgs","repos_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/repos","events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/events{/privacy}","received_events_url":"https://api.github.com/users/facero/received_events","type":"User","site_admin":false},"body":"
**Description**

This PR is to adress the issue #3710. It adds a short description of mamba in the `getting started` and  `dev` docs as an alternative to conda.
While mamba can also be used as a full replacement for conda (e.g. `mamba activate gammapy-0.19`), I prefer to stick to use mamba just for installation to reduce confusion in users mind ;-)


I also updated the dev doc (intro.rst) to mention using `conda activate` by default instead of `source activate`.
The switch in conda happened in 2017. I imagine most of users have conda > 4.4 today.

**Description**

This PR is to adress the issue #3710. It adds a short description of mamba in the `getting started` and  `dev` docs as an alternative to conda.
While mamba can also be used as a full replacement for conda (e.g. `mamba activate gammapy-0.19`), I prefer to stick to use mamba just for installation to reduce confusion in users mind ;-)


I also updated the dev doc (intro.rst) to mention using `conda activate` by default instead of `source activate`.
The switch in conda happened in 2017. I imagine most of users have conda > 4.4 today.

**Description**

This PR is to adress the issue #3710. It adds a short description of mamba in the `getting started` and  `dev` docs as an alternative to conda.
While mamba can also be used as a full replacement for conda (e.g. `mamba activate gammapy-0.19`), I prefer to stick to use mamba just for installation to reduce confusion in users mind ;-)


I also updated the dev doc (intro.rst) to mention using `conda activate` by default instead of `source activate`.
The switch in conda happened in 2017. I imagine most of users have conda > 4.4 today.

Here is on my laptop:
```
(base) 09:18-sap123 ~/$  mamba activate gammapy-dev
Run 'mamba init' to be able to run mamba activate/deactivate
and start a new shell session. Or use conda to activate/deactivate.
```
Once I did `mamba init`:
```
(base) 09:19-sap123 ~/$  mamba activate gammapy-dev
(gammapy-dev) 09:19-sapmcn177 ~/$
```

Although it's maybe simpler to stick to conda for everything and just use mamba for installation (but I guess its a question of taste).","reactions":{"url":"https://api.github.com/repos/gammapy/gammapy/issues/comments/1022958381/reactions","total_count":0,"+1":0,"-1":0,"laugh":0,"hooray":0,"confused":0,"heart":0,"rocket":0,"eyes":0},"performed_via_github_app":null}}

This PR adds a helper function in utils to transform a string, list/np array of energy Quantities to a string or list of strings of well formatted energies. Example: E = [1.54e2 *u.GeV, 4300 *u.keV, 300.6e12*u.eV] => ['154 GeV', '4.30 MeV', '301 TeV'] Example of before/After in plot_interactive: **Dear reviewer** This helper function has been applied to the `plot_interactive()` and `plot_grid()` functions to show more readable energies. The function could also be useful in other contexts but I started with these only 2 cases for now. Tests were added as well. One point I'm not sure is how to best check whether the input is iterable or not in `energy_unit_format()`. Possible inputs are list of quantities or a np.array of quantities. If I have just E = 1 *u.TeV I don't to return a list. Any suggestions to improve my first attempt ?
